years.2014: A high-volume year dominated by the Planning Board, with Highland Sand & Gravel appearing repeatedly for continuations on site plan/special permit relief and Woodbury Common surfacing as a recurring applicant. Activity clustered in R2A residential zones, with a mix of suburban area variances (pools, setbacks, additions) alongside a few larger commercial/industrial matters. Devenuto is already a frequent name in the record, foreshadowing a long-running professional presence.
years.2015: Volume stayed high but shifted slightly, with the IB district climbing as Cabela's and Woodbury Centre LLC signage/site plan matters drove commercial activity. The PB again carried the bulk of the docket while ZBA work leaned toward interpretations (e.g., the pawn-shop accessory-use ruling under 310-49). Smith Clove emerges as a recurring reference point, and the big-box/retail corridor is clearly the year's center of gravity.
years.2016: Docket composition tilted more toward the ZBA than in prior years, with R1A residential variances (second dwelling units, accessory-use fights, pool/fence setbacks) becoming the dominant texture. Woodbury Common continues as a steady PB presence, and Devenuto mentions spike sharply (34), suggesting heavy representation/engineering involvement across many files. Use-variance fights around B&B event space (Pulver) and guest-house conversions (Spiegel) are a notable sub-theme.
years.2017: A sharp collapse in volume (42 apps) — thin year, especially on the Planning Board side (only 13). The ZBA carried the year, with AEONN's hotel frontage/interpretation saga and Central Valley Properties' mixed-use building dominating continuations. The data is thin enough that trends should be read cautiously, but the record reads like a year of a few big, slow-moving contested files rather than routine throughput.
years.2018: Extremely thin year and an outlier: only 18 entries, all ZBA, with no Planning Board records surfaced at all — likely a data-capture gap rather than a true absence of PB work. Substantively, the ZBA was occupied with fence-height and stockade disputes (Torres, Sofer, O&R Melody Lane) and continuing mixed-use and non-frontage lot questions. Devenuto again appears heavily across the small docket.
years.2019: Volume rebounds to a more normal split between PB and ZBA. R2A residential variances dominate, and a new repeat name — Hartman — appears substantially (14 hits), marking the beginning of what becomes a multi-year pattern. Recurring themes include place-of-worship/shul conversions (Brach & Mann), Rushmore/Pulver winery access, and non-frontage single-family lots.
years.2020: COVID-year dip to 75 applications, with the PB outpacing the ZBA. Woodbury Common remains a steady commercial presence (Polo outdoor seating, Shops of Woodbury, PARM), and the board spent meaningful time on procedural/code revisions (notice radius, §A314 amendments) rather than only individual applications. Residential variance work was routine (pools, fences, signage for Aldi).
years.2021: Back up to 136 applications, with Highland Mills overtaking Woodbury as the top locality — a geographic shift that persists. Hartman jumps to 28 mentions and Woodbury Villas emerges (10), while variance requests (especially fence and non-conforming expansion cases like Yitzchok Stein/Ideal Design) proliferate. Beer World's signage/size variance and multiple place-of-worship conversions signal intensifying commercial and religious-use pressure.
years.2022: A near-record 154 applications, heavily PB-weighted, with LC zoning climbing to the top district as Dunkin drive-thru interpretation and Beer World continuations play out. Hartman mentions surge to 48, reflecting a design firm now deeply embedded in the ARB pipeline across multiple First Avenue properties. Interpretations and appeals tick up as a request type, suggesting more contested code-reading questions.
years.2023: Peak volume year at 199 applications, with Central Valley overtaking the other hamlets as the busiest locality and HB (highway business) leading zoning districts. Hartman (60) and Morgante (24) dominate repeat-player mentions, and variance requests explode to 41 — the docket is visibly more contested. Major files include Woodbury Common Phase V expansion SEQRA scoping, 50 Smith Clove LLC, and a wave of ARB/shul and second-dwelling-unit matters.
years.2024: Volume cools from the 2023 peak to 130, but Highland Mills solidifies as the top locality and variance requests remain elevated (27). The repeat-player bench broadens: Hartman (40), Morgante (29), Gelb (21), Barshov (13), and Woodbury Common (16) all register heavily, indicating a stable stable of professionals working the boards. Notable files include Speyside quarry special permit, Woodview Heights ridge preservation, and the KJ/Palm Tree emergency access road — larger regional-impact matters.
years.2025: Back up to 167 applications with the PB carrying roughly three-quarters of the load. Hartman mentions leap to 93 — by far the highest single-player concentration in the 12-year record — while Highland Mills R-1A residential work dominates zoning. The docket mixes national-brand signage (Aspen Dental, Starbucks, Chipotle, Extra Space Storage) with continuing big files (Woodview Heights, Woodbury Townhouses, Speyside, Congregation Gemilas shul).

prediction_2026: Based on the trajectory, expect 2026 volume to land in the 150–190 range, continuing the post-2020 pattern of PB-heavy dockets running roughly 2.5–3× the ZBA. Highland Mills and Central Valley should remain the busiest localities, with R-1A and R-2A residential variances plus HB/IB commercial signage and site-plan work as the backbone. Hartman's design practice is likely to remain the single most frequent professional touchpoint, alongside continued appearances by Morgante, Barshov, Gelb, and Woodbury Common representatives. Watch for Woodbury Common Phase V, Woodview Heights, Woodbury Townhouses, and Speyside to keep generating continuations, and for place-of-worship/shul conversions and second-dwelling-unit special permits to remain a steady sub-docket. Variance and interpretation requests will probably stay elevated relative to pre-2022 baselines, reflecting a code that is being tested repeatedly on fence heights, impervious coverage, non-conforming expansions, and mixed-use definitions.

demographics.framing: The Village of Woodbury sits directly adjacent to the Village of Palm Tree (formerly Kiryas Joel), and the application record in its Planning Board and Zoning Board of Appeals minutes reflects that adjacency. This analysis tracks three categories in the application text: religious-use markers (e.g., 'shul,' 'mikvah/mikveh,' 'place of worship,' 'place of assembly,' 'synagogue'), household-adaptation variance terms (e.g., 'accessory apartment,' 'second kitchen,' 'two-family,' 'in-law,' 'bungalow,' 'additional bedroom'), and explicit cross-community references to Kiryas Joel, KJ, or Palm Tree. The data reflects filings, hearings, and excerpted application language — not information about who resides in the Village. Keyword counts are raw occurrences and should be read as a signal of what appears in application text, not a direct measure of development volume or population.

years.2014: Religious-use language appears primarily around a single clustered matter — the Schunnemunk Holdings / Cataggio 'place of worship' special-permit and variance filings at 406 Schunnemunk Road (8 'place of worship' hits, 1 'shul'). Household-adaptation signal is thin, limited to one 'accessory apartment' reference tied to an office-building variance. No explicit Kiryas Joel or Palm Tree references appear in the excerpted text this year.
years.2015: No household-adaptation terms and only one 'shul' hit, but cross-community references emerge: seven 'kiryas joel' mentions appearing in Planning Board discussion of a water-treatment/aqueduct project and a ZBA matter referencing United Talmudical Academy of Kiryas Joel. The religious-use signal is weak this year; the demographic-coded content is dominated by the cross-jurisdictional infrastructure filing.
years.2016: Cross-community references remain prominent (7 'kiryas joel' + 3 'kj' hits) and are again concentrated in the Village of KJ water pump station / water treatment facility site plan review at Seven Springs Road and Ridge Road. Religious-use language is thin (1 'shul' hit). A first household-adaptation marker appears in a ZBA matter (Zucker, 22 Cindy Lane) referencing a 'second kitchen,' alongside one 'in-law' reference.
years.2017: Total application volume drops sharply (42 applications) and the demographic-coded signal is weak across all three categories. Cross-community content is limited to a handful of 'kiryas joel' / 'kj' references tied to an emergency access road filing and a wetlands-mitigation discussion. No religious-use or household-adaptation hits.
years.2018: Application volume is at its lowest in the series (18 applications) and the signal is correspondingly thin. One 'kiryas joel' reference appears in a ZBA matter concerning a multi-family project in that village, and one 'additional bedroom' reference appears in the Brach & Mann / 195 Bakertown Road filing. No religious-use keyword hits.
years.2019: Religious-use language returns at a noticeably higher level: 13 'shul' hits, 7 'mikvah' hits, and 1 'religious use' reference, concentrated in several distinct filings (Brach & Mann / 24 Catskill High Rail, Congregation Ohev Shalom / 230 Schunnemunk Road, Weiss / 26 Apple Hill). Multiple filings describe proposed conversion of existing single-family dwellings to shul-and-mikvah use. One 'two-family' household-adaptation reference (Falkowitz / 12 Skytop Road) and no cross-community hits this year.
years.2020: Religious-use signal reaches its highest density in the series relative to application volume: 33 'shul,' 9 'mikveh,' 7 'mikvah,' 6 'place of worship,' 3 'synagogue,' and 2 'religious use' hits across 75 applications. The filings are concentrated in a small number of matters (Congregation Ohev Shalom at 230 Schunnemunk, Friedman at 32 Blueberry Lane, Hadley Farms LLC at 47 Hadley Farm Road), all involving proposed conversion of single-family dwellings to shul / shul-with-mikveh use. No cross-community or household-adaptation keyword hits this year.
years.2021: All three categories are active: religious-use terms (11 'shul,' 3 'mikvah,' 3 'place of worship,' 1 'religious use'), cross-community references (1 'kiryas joel' + 4 'kj' tied to the KJ Water Treatment Facility at 147 Seven Springs Rd), and household-adaptation terms (2 'two-family,' 3 'in-law,' 1 'bungalow'). Religious-use filings include continued review of Friedman/Blueberry and Hadley Farms, plus a Cody Boys School 'school/mikvah' addition. Household-adaptation matters include Abraham/14 Skytop (three-family to two-family conversion), Balsome/Yenchik (mother-in-law use), and Stein (bungalow expansion).
years.2022: The highest year for cross-community references (5 'kiryas joel' + 8 'kj' hits), driven by the ongoing KJ Water Treatment Facility and KJ Well 33 review at 147 Seven Springs Rd. Religious-use language shifts toward 'place of worship' (13) and 'place of assembly' (3), the latter clustered around the 14 Castleton Drive enforcement matter. Household-adaptation signal is the strongest of the series (2 'two-family,' 2 'in-law,' 2 'additional bedroom,' 6 'bungalow'), with the Achdus bungalow complex on Schunnemunk Road and several Hartman-designed filings appearing in the record.
years.2023: Religious-use language remains elevated (14 'mikvah,' 11 'shul,' 1 'place of worship,' 1 'place of assembly,' 1 'religious use') across a higher application volume (199). Much of the signal is concentrated in a cluster of Hartman-designed single-family-dwelling filings (22-24 Catskill High Rail, 88 Jefferson, 60 Southfield, 2 First Avenue) describing shul additions and private mikvah additions. Household-adaptation terms include 2 'two-family,' 3 'additional bedroom,' and 1 'bungalow'; no cross-community keyword hits this year.
years.2024: Religious-use hits drop (2 'mikvah,' 2 'shul'), concentrated in the Congregation Gemilas filing at 24 Southfield Falls (single-family to shul-with-mikvah conversion in the senior-housing section of Woodbury Villas). The first 'palm tree' reference appears in the excerpted text, alongside 1 'kiryas joel' hit, both tied to the KJ Emergency Access Road / Acres & Dinev filing. Household-adaptation signal is weak (1 'multi-family' tied to a Hartman duplex filing at 49 & 51 Southfield Falls, 1 'bungalow' tied to Achdus Unit B3).
years.2025: Religious-use language rebounds moderately (11 'shul,' 3 'mikveh'), driven largely by continued review of Congregation Gemilas / 24 Southfield Falls. Cross-community references are active (1 'kiryas joel,' 1 'palm tree,' 5 'kj'), appearing in the KJ Emergency Access Road filing (now described as extending from Dinev Rd. and Frankfurter Rd. in the Village of Palm Tree) and in Hartman Design / 19 Seven Springs Mountain Road discussions of water-supply source. Household-adaptation terms are limited to 3 'bungalow' hits, all from the Achdus Unit B3 filing on Schunnemunk Road.

demographics.outlook_2026: Based on the 2014–2025 trajectory, 2026 filings are likely to continue showing all three demographic-coded signal categories, with religious-use markers ('shul,' 'mikvah/mikveh') remaining the most consistent feature of the record since 2019. Cross-community references are likely to persist and to increasingly use 'Palm Tree' alongside or in place of 'Kiryas Joel' / 'KJ,' following the naming shift first observed in 2024–2025, and are likely to remain concentrated in the KJ/Palm Tree Water Treatment Facility, Well 33, and Emergency Access Road matters. Household-adaptation filings (bungalow, two-family, in-law, additional bedroom) will likely continue appearing at the modest pace seen since 2021, with the Achdus bungalow complex and Hartman-designed additions as recurring case types. Conversion filings — single-family dwellings proposed for shul or shul-with-mikvah use — are the most distinctive pattern of the 2019–2025 window and should be expected to continue appearing on Planning Board agendas. Readers should note this is an extrapolation from application-text keywords only; it describes the likely shape of the filing record, not the composition of the Village's residents.
